frameworks.name: Privacy Threat Model
frameworks.description: Privacy is not binary. Define who you are protecting against — that determines which controls matter. Misidentifying your adversary wastes effort on irrelevant controls while leaving real threats unaddressed.
steps: Identify your adversary: data brokers, advertisers, stalkers, competitors, state actors, or opportunistic criminals — each requires different controls
Identify the data you are protecting: identity, location, communications, financial, professional, relational
Map which adversaries can realistically access which data categories given current exposure
Rate each intersection: likelihood × harm if exposed
Select controls proportionate to your actual threat model — not maximum paranoia, not naive defaults
Review quarterly: threat model changes as life circumstances change

examples.adversary: Motivated individual aggregating public data sources
examples.data_at_risk: Home address, family members, daily routines
examples.proportionate_controls: PO box for all public registrations, opt-out from data brokers, separate professional identity from personal, location services off for social media

examples.data_at_risk: Business strategy, key relationships, travel patterns
examples.proportionate_controls: Minimal public social media, travel booking via corporate account not personal, encrypted communications for sensitive discussions

antipatterns: Maximum privacy controls regardless of actual threat — creates friction without proportionate benefit
No threat model — applying random controls based on articles read
Treating privacy as a one-time setup rather than ongoing practice
Ignoring the human layer — most privacy failures are social, not technical

frameworks.name: Data Minimization Protocol
frameworks.description: Data that does not exist cannot be breached, subpoenaed, or sold. The most effective privacy control is not generating or retaining unnecessary data in the first place.
steps: Audit what data you currently generate: accounts, purchases, location, communications, browsing
For each data type ask: does retaining this serve me, or only the platform collecting it?
Delete: accounts not used in 90+ days, stored data with no ongoing value
Minimize at collection: use aliases where possible, provide minimum required fields, avoid optional data fields
Separate identities: professional, personal, high-privacy activities should not share accounts or contact information
Prefer ephemeral: use services that do not retain data by default where the function allows
antipatterns: Convenience accounts linking identity across unrelated services (login with Google/Facebook)
Providing real name, phone, and address where a pseudonym and PO box would work
Retaining communications indefinitely — most should be deleted after their purpose expires
Single email address used everywhere — one breach exposes everything

frameworks.name: Communications Security Tiering
frameworks.description: Not all communications require the same protection. Tiering matches security overhead to sensitivity, preventing both under-protection of sensitive content and over-engineering of routine communication.
steps: Tier 1 — Public: assume anything sent here is public. Social media, public forums, standard email to unknown parties.
Tier 2 — Private but not sensitive: standard encrypted email (ProtonMail/Tutanota), encrypted messaging (Signal). Daily professional communication.
Tier 3 — Sensitive: Signal with disappearing messages, in-person, or air-gapped communication. Legal strategy, financial planning, confidential business.
Tier 4 — Critical: in-person only, no devices present. Matters where compromise would be catastrophic.
Assign each regular communication context to a tier
Ensure you have a functioning tool for each tier you need
antipatterns: Using Tier 1 tools for Tier 3 content — SMS for legal strategy, Gmail for confidential financials
Encrypting everything regardless of sensitivity — friction kills adoption
Encrypted tool with unencrypted backup — the backup is the vulnerability
Discussing Tier 3 content with device present in the room (microphone risk)

frameworks.name: Identity Separation Architecture
frameworks.description: Separate identities for separate contexts limits correlation. When identities merge, a single breach or data sale exposes the complete picture. Separation requires deliberate architecture.
steps: Define your identity contexts: public professional, private personal, high-privacy, financial
Assign a separate email address to each context — not aliases, separate accounts on separate providers
Use separate browsers or browser profiles per context — prevent cross-context tracking via cookies
Separate payment methods: business card, personal card, privacy-preserving option for sensitive purchases
Never use real name + home address for public-facing registrations — PO box and professional name
Review annually: have any contexts merged inadvertently?
antipatterns: Single email for all contexts — one breach exposes all contexts
Same username across platforms — trivial to correlate
Home address as billing address for public-facing services
Social media account linked to professional identity containing personal content

prompts.name: Personal Privacy Threat Model
prompts.template: Build my privacy threat model. My public exposure: [describe — social media, professional profile, business ownership, etc.]. My primary concerns: [doxxing / competitive intelligence / data brokers / other]. Map my realistic adversaries to the data they could access, rate each risk, and give me the top 3 proportionate controls.

prompts.name: Data Minimization Audit
prompts.template: Audit my digital data footprint. Accounts I know about: [list]. Data I regularly generate: [location, purchases, communications, browsing — describe]. Identify my highest-exposure data categories, what I should delete, and where I should minimize at collection going forward.

prompts.name: Communications Security Setup
prompts.template: Design my communications security architecture. Current tools I use: [list]. Regular communication contexts: [professional, personal, sensitive business, legal, financial — describe]. Assign each context to a security tier and identify any gaps where I am using under-protected tools for sensitive content.

prompts.name: Identity Separation Review
prompts.template: Review my identity separation. Current email accounts: [list with purposes]. Payment methods: [list]. Public registrations using my home address: [list]. Identify where my identities have merged inappropriately and give me a prioritized separation plan.

prompts.name: Data Broker Opt-Out Plan
prompts.template: I want to remove myself from data broker databases. My public exposure: [name, location, professional profile — describe]. Give me a prioritized list of data broker categories to opt out from, starting with the highest-risk aggregators, and the process for each.

examples.title: Public Figure Doxxing Prevention
examples.context: Content creator with 50K followers receiving harassment, home address at risk
examples.framework_applied: Privacy Threat Model + Identity Separation Architecture
examples.findings: Home address on business registration (public record), same email for public contact and personal banking, social media photos with identifiable location metadata
interventions: Registered agent service replaces home address on all business filings
PO box for all public-facing mail
Separate email: public@domain.com for fans, private account for banking/legal
Location metadata stripped from all photos before posting
Opt-out submitted to top 20 data brokers
examples.outcome: Home address removed from public record within 60 days. Identity separation prevents future correlation.

examples.title: Executive Communications Hardening
examples.context: CEO discussing acquisition strategy over standard email and SMS
examples.diagnosis: Tier 3 content (M&A strategy, legal discussions) transmitted via Tier 1 tools (standard email, SMS). Single breach exposes competitive strategy.
examples.intervention: Signal with disappearing messages for board and legal counsel. ProtonMail for confidential vendor discussions. Standard email for routine operations only.
examples.outcome: Sensitive communications isolated to encrypted channels with no persistent storage.

vocabulary.definition: Identification of who you are protecting against and what data matters — determines which controls are proportionate.
Generating and retaining only the data necessary for a specific purpose. Data that does not exist cannot be compromised.
Maintaining distinct, non-correlated identities for different life contexts to limit exposure from any single breach.
Intelligence gathered from publicly available sources. What an adversary can learn about you without any privileged access.
Data about data. Photos contain location and device metadata; emails contain routing and timing metadata. Often more revealing than content.
Companies that aggregate and sell personal data from public records, purchase history, and tracked behavior. Primary source of doxxing data.
Discipline of identifying and protecting sensitive information from adversarial collection. Originally military; applies to any high-stakes context.
Linking data points across separate sources to identify a person or their behavior. Separation prevents correlation.
Service that receives official correspondence on behalf of a business, keeping the owner's home address off public business filings.
Encrypted messaging feature that auto-deletes messages after a set period, limiting data retention and legal exposure.
Physically isolated from any network. Used for highest-sensitivity communications where even encrypted digital channels are insufficient.
Operating under a consistent but non-real-name identity. Protects personal identity while maintaining accountability within a context.

sources.title: The Art of Invisibility
sources.relevance: Practical OPSEC, identity separation, data minimization

sources.title: Permanent Record
sources.relevance: Surveillance architecture, metadata risks, communications security

sources.title: Michael Bazzell's OSINT Techniques
sources.relevance: Data broker removal, identity separation, doxxing prevention

v78_consciousnesses: Constraint Mapper (hard privacy laws vs self-imposed exposure)
Rogan v2.1 (realistic threat assessment — calibrated, not paranoid)
House v2.1 (differential diagnosis of privacy failure vectors)
master_laws: Law 6 (Defense — security is a state of being)

4_gate_relevance.gate_1_safe: Law 2 (Silence) enforcement — Privacy Shield active; no PII leakage
4_gate_relevance.gate_4_aligned: Law 7 (Ownership) — all outputs respect Sovereign's data sovereignty

4_gate_validation.safe: PASS — defensive privacy practices only; no surveillance or offensive techniques
4_gate_validation.true: PASS — grounded in established OPSEC practice and EFF guidance
4_gate_validation.high_leverage: PASS — identity separation and data minimization prevent the majority of realistic privacy failures
4_gate_validation.aligned: PASS — directly serves Security need; Law 2 and Law 7 compliance
